coredata.dc:title: Heterojunction and co-catalyst engineering of bismuth vanadate by nickel molybdenum oxide and carbon quantum dot decorations for photoelectrochemical water splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kuo, Tsung-Rong
Chiou, Yu-Tai
Huang, Hsiao-Wen
Kongvarhodom, Chutima
Saukani, Muhammad
Yougbaré, Sibidou
Chen, Hung-Ming
Lin, Lu-Yin
coredata.dc:description: Improving the charge diffusion length and water oxidation kinetics of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is essential to achieve better photoelectrochemical catalytic ability for water oxidation. Establishing heterojunctions and incorporating co-catalysts with BiVO4 create facile charge transfer paths and generate more water oxidation reactions. In this work, nickel molybdenum oxide (NM) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) are firstly deposited on BiVO4 by hydrothermal and soaking processes to design an efficient co-catalyst/photocatalyst system for water oxidation. The depositing amount of CQD is optimized to balance contributions of CQD and NM as the hole sink and the light-to-electron generator, respectively. The highest photocurrent densities of 2.2 and 3.9 mA/cm2 are achieved for BiVO4/NM/CQD in the electrolyte without and with hole scavenger, respectively. The BiVO4 electrode only shows a photocurrent density of 1.2 mA/cm2 in the electrolyte without hole scavenger. The best photoelectrochemical catalytic ability of BiVO4/NM/CQD is approached by the reduced charge-transfer resistance and charge recombination as well as the enhancement on carrier densities. The excellent long-term stability with the photocurrent retention of 90% under illumination for 24 h is also obtained for BiVO4/NM/CQD. This study opens a blueprint on designing efficient catalysts for water oxidation by incorporating heterojunction and co-catalyst materials simultaneously.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
Carbon quantum dot
Co-catalyst
Heterojunction
Nickel molybdenum oxide
Water oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of BiVO4/NiMoO4/carbon quantum dots electrodes
Physical analysis of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD
Electrochemical analysis of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan , under Grant 111-2221-E-027-071-MY3 . This work is also assisted by Precision Analysis and Material Research Center, NTUT .

This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan, under Grant 111-2221-E-027-071-MY3. This work is also assisted by Precision Analysis and Material Research Center, NTUT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) BVO, (b) BVO/NM, and BVO/NM/CQD with the CQD concentrations of (c) 2, (d) 4, (e) 6, (f) 8 and (g) 16 mg/ml; (h) the TEM image of CQD and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml).

(a) XRD patterns of BVO/NM (0 mg/ml) and BVO/NM/CQD with different CQD concentrations; (b) XRD patterns of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml); (c) XRD pattern of BVO/NM; (d,e) Raman spectra of BVO/NM (0 mg/ml) and BVO/NM/CQD with different CQD concentrations.
(a) Bi 4f, (b) V 2p and (c) O 1s spectra of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD; (d) Ni 2p and (e) Mo 3d spectra of BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD; (f) C 1s spectra of BVO/NM/CQD.
(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) Tauc plot of BVO/NM (0 mg/ml) and BVO/NM/CQD with different CQD concentrations; (c) UV–vis spectra and (d) Tauc plot of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml).

(a) LSV curves at 20 mV/s under illumination, (b) Mott-Schottky plot, (c) Nyquist plot at 1.23 VRHE under illumination and (d) equivalent circuit of BVO/NM (0 mg/ml) and BVO/NM/CQD with different CQD concentrations.

(a) Transient current plot at 1.23 VRHE, (b) LSV curves, and (c) Nyquist plot of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml) electrodes in the electrolyte containing 0.5 M Na2SO4 and 0.1 M PBS; (d) transient current plot at 1.23 VRHE, (e) LSV curves, and (f) Nyquist plot of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml) electrodes in the electrolyte containing 0.5 M Na2SO4 and 0.5 M Na2SO3.

(a) Mott-Schottky plots of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes; (b) PL spectra of BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes and (c) photocurrent density as function of time measured under light illumination for 24 h; (d) The SEM and TEM images of BVO/NM/CQD electrode after stability test and (e) XRD pattern of BVO/NM/CQD electrode before and after stability test.
Illustration of experiments for synthesizing CQD solution and BVO/NM/CQD.
Illustration of band structure of BVO/NM/CQD and charge transfer path of water oxidation.

head.ce:title: Heterojunction and co-catalyst engineering of bismuth vanadate by nickel molybdenum oxide and carbon quantum dot decorations for photoelectrochemical water splitting

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to the manuscript.

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling editor: Dr M Mahdi Najafpour

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improving the charge diffusion length and water oxidation kinetics of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is essential to achieve better photoelectrochemical catalytic ability for water oxidation. Establishing heterojunctions and incorporating co-catalysts with BiVO4 create facile charge transfer paths and generate more water oxidation reactions. In this work, nickel molybdenum oxide (NM) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) are firstly deposited on BiVO4 by hydrothermal and soaking processes to design an efficient co-catalyst/photocatalyst system for water oxidation. The depositing amount of CQD is optimized to balance contributions of CQD and NM as the hole sink and the light-to-electron generator, respectively. The highest photocurrent densities of 2.2 and 3.9 mA/cm2 are achieved for BiVO4/NM/CQD in the electrolyte without and with hole scavenger, respectively. The BiVO4 electrode only shows a photocurrent density of 1.2 mA/cm2 in the electrolyte without hole scavenger. The best photoelectrochemical catalytic ability of BiVO4/NM/CQD is approached by the reduced charge-transfer resistance and charge recombination as well as the enhancement on carrier densities. The excellent long-term stability with the photocurrent retention of 90% under illumination for 24 h is also obtained for BiVO4/NM/CQD. This study opens a blueprint on designing efficient catalysts for water oxidation by incorporating heterojunction and co-catalyst materials simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is considered as the most clean method for generating hydrogen which is one of chemical fuels with high energy density [1–3]. The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) happened in the photoanode proceeds the four-electron transfer path [4–8]. Hence, the OER is regarded to be more sluggish than hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) occurred in the photocathode [9–11]. Developing efficient catalysts to accelerate OER can essentially improve the photoelectrochemical performance of the PEC water splitting system [12–17].

Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is one of the efficient photocatalysts toward OER because of the suitable band edges and the small band gap, but the sluggish surface/bulk charge separation and partial visible photon-responsive behavior of BiVO4 reduce its solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency [18–20]. Several manners were proposed to solve these issues for enhancing the photoelectrochemical performance of BiVO4 [21–27]. Establishing type II heterojunction can separate charges more efficiently by creating staggered offset nanoscale charge cascade [28–31]. Chen et al. used the solution process to fabricate BiVO4 and Mo-doped BiVO4 heterojunction for the application of the photocatalyst for OER [32]. Liu and co-authors fabricated the TiO2 and BiVO4 heterojunction using an in-situ method for accelerating photoelectrochemical catalytic OER [33]. Lai and co-workers used the alkaline etching technique to establish the BiVO4 and BiOx heterojunction for the application of the photocatalyst for OER [22]. Nickel molybdenum oxide (NiMoO4) is one of the excellent non-noble catalysts having the high oxidation state density, good stability, easy fabrication and eco-friendly properties [34–37]. Gao and co-workers incorporated NiMoO4 in the BiVO4 electrode to establish type II heterojunction for PEC water splitting system [38]. However, the discussion on the electrocatalytic ability and main functions in the BiVO4 electrode is limited for NiMoO4. On the other hand, depositing co-catalyst in BiVO4 is another manner for enhancing the photoelectrochemical catalytic ability via providing more active sites and hole sinks [39,40]. Carbon quantum dot (CQD) is a newly proposed material with superior electron transfer and reservoir capabilities, which promote it as an efficient material on the catalysis field. The CQD is reported to broaden light absorption and facilitate charge separate/transfer when incorporated with photocatalysts [41]. Hu et al. modifies BiVO4 with CQD through a hydrothermal process. As compared to the pristine BiVO4, the CQD and BiVO4 composite shows enhanced visible-light activity toward degradation of benzyl paraben pollution [42]. Liu et al. modified BiVO4 with carbon nitride quantum dots and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots to form heterojunctions for improving the photocatalytic performance [43]. Wu and co-workers fabricated carbon dot and BiVO4 quantum dot composites with improved photocatalytic activity and high stability for overall water splitting [44]. However, the studies for CQD and BiVO4 composites are still limited. Therefore, it is promising to apply the heterojunction and co-catalyst techniques for enhancing the photoelectrochemical catalytic ability of BiVO4 toward water oxidation.

In this work, the NiMoO4 and CQD were firstly combined with BiVO4 to establish heterojunction and co-catalyst/photocatalyst system for accelerating water oxidation reaction by using the hydrothermal and soaking techniques. Different concentrations of CQD were applied to fabricate the BiVO4/NiMoO4/CQD electrodes. The morphology, crystallinity and optical properties of BiVO4, BiVO4/NiMoO4 and BiVO4/NiMoO4/CQD electrodes prepared using different CQD concentrations are analyzed. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Mott-Schottky plots and Nyquist plot of BiVO4, BiVO4/NiMoO4 and BiVO4/NiMoO4/CQD electrodes were also measured to discuss the effects of CQD concentration as well as the incorporation of NiMoO4 and CQD on the photoelectrochemical performances of BiVO4. The long-term stability under continuous light illumination for the optimal BiVO4/NiMoO4/CQD electrode was also estimated. This work provides detailed analysis on the physical and electrochemical properties for BiVO4 with NiMoO4 and CQD depositions. The electrolytes with and without the hole scavenger were also applied to evaluate the photoelectrochemical performance of the co-catalyst/photocatalyst systems. In brief, the main objectives of this research work are to establish heterojunction and incorporate a co-catalyst in the BiVO4 system for enhancing the photoelectrochemical catalytic ability toward water oxidation reaction. The main strategies are to deposit NiMoO4 and CQD on the BiVO4 electrode and to discuss the depositing amounts of CQD on te material and electrochemical properties. The chemistry behind the BiVO4/NiMoO4/CQD includes the suitable relative band edges of BiVO4 and NiMoO4, and the higher electrical conductivity of CQD. The suitable band edge positions of BiVO4 and NiMoO4 are favorable for inducing efficient charge cascade, and the high conductivity of CQD is beneficial of attracting holes for generating water splitting.

The BiOI film was firstly fabricated on the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass as the precursor of BiVO4. The FTO glass was cleaned by neutral cleaner, deionized water (DIW), ethanol and acetone nitrile in ultrasonication for 15 min, separately [21]. The BiOI film is coated on FTO glass using the electrodeposition technique in a three-electrode system, in which the FTO glass was acted as the working electrode, the platinum wire was acted as the counter electrode, and the Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) was acted as the reference electrode. The potential and duration for the electrodeposition process are respectively set at −0.1 VAg/AgCl and 3 min. The electrodeposition electrolyte was prepared by mixing two solutions. One of the solutions contains 3.32 g of KI (Showa, 99.5%) and 0.97 g of Bi(NO)3·5H2O (Alfa Aesar, 98%) in DIW with the pH value of 1.7. The other solution contains 0.497 g of p-bezoquinone (Alfa Aesar, 98+%) in 20.00 ml of absolute ethanol. The BiVO4 electrode was fabricated by the drop casting technique followed by the annealing process. The solution for drop casting contains 0.20 M acetylacetone vanadium oxide (Acros Organics, 99%) in 5.00 ml of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, J.T.Baker, 99.9%). The solution was dropped on the BiOI film to fully cover surface. The following annealing process was carried out at 450 °C for 2 h (heating rate is 2 °C/min). The residual V2O5 was then removed by treating the electrodes in 1.00 M NaOH (Fluka, 98+%) [45].

The NiMoO4 was deposited on the BVO surface by using the hydrothermal technique. The precursor solution was obtained with 0.87 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (99.0%, Acros) and 0.73 g of Na2MoO4 · 2H2O (99.0+%, Acros) in 50 ml of DIW. The as-prepared solution (12.5 ml) and the BVO electrode (facing downwards) are transferred to the Teflon liner, which is then put in a stainless-steel autoclave for carrying out the hydrothermal process at 90 °C for 30 min. The electrodes are following annealed at 450 °C for 3 h in air (heating rate is 2 °C/min). The BiVO4/NiMoO4 (BVO/NM) electrode was then obtained. The experimental process of BiVO4/NiMoO4 was shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of BiVO4/NiMoO4/carbon quantum dots electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon quantum dot (CQD) solution was firstly prepared by dissolving 1.5 g of sucrose (Showa) in 50 ml of DIW at room temperature. The solution was put into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 300 min. After cooling the autoclave to the room temperature, the solution was centrifuged with 4000 revolutions per 30 min. The CQDs solution was then obtained in the top solution with the concentration of 16 mg/ml. The BiVO4/NiMoO4/CQD (BVO/NM/CQD) electrode was prepared by soaking the BVO/NM electrodes into the CQDs solution for 6 h. The different CQD solution concentrations of 2, 4, 6, 8 and 16 mg/ml were applied. The experimental process of BiVO4/NiMoO4/carbon quantum dots was shown in Scheme 1.

The surface morphology of BVO, BVO/NM, and BVO/NM/CQD are examined by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Nova NanoSEM 230, FEI, Oregon, USA) and transmission scanning electron microscopy. The compositions and phases of BVO, BVO/NM, and BVO/NM/CQD are examined using X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, X'Pert3 Powder, PANalytical) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG Scientific ESCALAB 250) measurement with the Al Ka radiation. The light absorbance features of BVO, BVO/NM, and BVO/NM/CQD was measured via ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis, JASCO V750). The electrochemical performance of BVO, BVO/NM, and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes is measured in a three-electrode system. The Pt wire was acted as the counter electrode and the Ag/AgCl electrode was acted as the reference electrode. The simulated sunlight is the 300-Watt Xe lamp equipped with AM 1.5G filter (Newport). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was evaluated by potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT 204, Autolab, Eco–Chemie, the Netherlands) equipped with the FRA2 module. The potentials are calculated versus reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) via Equation (1) as follows [46].
(1) E (vs. RHE) = E (vs. Ag/AgCl) + 0.05916 × 7 + 0.197 = E (vs. Ag/AgCl) + 0.611

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical analysis of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the influences of depositing nickel molybdenum oxide and CQD on the morphology of electrodes, the SEM images of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD were firstly examined. Fig. 1 (a) shows the SEM image of BVO, which shows particle-connected sheets vertically standing on the substrate. The diameter of particles is around 150 nm. After depositing nickel molybdenum oxide (Fig. 1(b)), the particles become larger (around 300 nm for the diameter) and fused with each other. The sheet-like structure of pristine BVO has vanished. Instead, the folded rod-like are appeared after the nickel molybdenum oxide deposition. Several wrinkles were also grown on the surface of these rod-like structures. Furthermore, the morphology of BVO/NM/CQD prepared using different concentrations of CQD was examined (Fig. 1(c)–(g)). A thin film was covered on the rod-like structure for all BVO/NM/CQD electrodes. The silk-like structure was also found between rods. The thin film and silk-like structure are inferred to be the connected CQD. To have closer observation, the TEM image of BVO/NM/CQD prepared using 4 mg/ml was examined, as shown in Fig. 1(h). The inserted figure shows the morphology of CQD, which presents a size less than 50 nm. The BVO/NM/CQD is composed of rods with a diameter of about 80 nm covered by tiny particles well distributed on the surface. The TEM image implies the successful coverage of CQD on the BVO/NM surface.

The compositions of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD were then examined using the XRD patterns. Fig. 2 (a) shows the XRD patterns of BVO/NM (0 mg/ml) and BVO/NM/CQD with different CQD concentrations. The standard patterns of monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS #14–0688) [47] and NiMoO4 (JCPDS #86–0361) [48] were also shown in this figure for comparison. The peaks corresponding to FTO attributed from the substrate were indicated in this figure. All patterns are highly similar to the standard pattern of monoclinic BiVO4 regardless of the deposition of CQD, implying the crystallinity of BVO is barely changed with NiMoO4 and CQD depositions. Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD patterns of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD. Similar patterns corresponding to monoclinic BiVO4 were again observed for all samples. The decorations of NiMoO4 and CQD are hard to verify using these two XRD patterns. Only the successful growth of BVO and maintenance of its crystallinity was verified. Since the peaks corresponding to NiMoO4 are not clear, an enlarged pattern for BVO/NM was then presented in Fig. 2(c) for clearer comparison. The standard pattern of NiMoO4 (JCPDS #86–0361) was also presented in this figure. The peaks corresponding to the NiMoO4 were observed at 2 theta values of 14.0o, 23.5o, 25.7o, 32.6o, 47.4o, 53.6o, 61.6o, 65.6o, 74.7o, and 76.7o. The NiMoO4 was therefore verified to be successfully grow on the BVO surface. To further examine the deposition of CQD, the Raman spectra of BVO/NM (0 mg/ml) and BVO/NM/CQD prepared with different CQD concentrations were measured, as shown in Fig. 2(d)–(e). The features of BVO are observed in all patterns in Fig. 2(d), indicating no the successful growth of BVO using the electrodeposition technique. With closer observation at larger Raman shifts (Fig. 2(e)), the obvious peaks at 1240, 1310, 1395, 1535, and 1653 cm−1 were obtained in the patterns of BVO/NM/CQD [49]. These peaks are invisible in the pattern of BVO/NM. Also, the intensity of these peaks are increased in the pattern of BVO/NM/CQD prepared using higher CQD concentrations. The results from the Raman spectra strongly indicate the successful deposition of CQD on the BVO/NM surface by the simple soaking process at room temperature.

The detailed compositions and electron states of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml) are further analyzed using the XPS spectra. Fig. 3 (a)-(c) shows the Bi 4f, V 2p and O 1s spectra of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD, respectively. The Bi 4f spectra of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD present two distinct peaks corresponding to 4f3/2 and 4f5/2. The binding energy of these peaks shifted to smaller values after depositing NiMoO4 on BVO. Furthermore, with the CQD deposition, the binding energy of these peaks shifts toward the smaller values than BVO/NM. This phenomenon is due to the change of local chemical environment for bismuth with the NiMoO4 and CQD depositions. Due to the smaller electronegativity of the depositing elements, the electron density around the bismuth atom increases and therefore the binding energy decreases [50]. The V 2p spectra of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD show two peaks corresponding to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbitals. The similar phenomenon as Bi 1s spectra was observed. The binding energy of these peaks shift toward smaller values after depositing NiMoO4 and CQD. The chemical environment around V atoms is also changed by the coating of NiMoO4 and CQD. The O 1s spectra of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD are not similar, but three peaks were fitted in all spectra, including the chemical adsorbed oxygen (Oad), oxygen vacancy (OV) and metal to oxygen bounds (M − O). The OV is inferred to play as the active sites for carrying out the water oxidation reactions. A much larger peak corresponding to OV was observed in the spectrum of BVO/NM/CQD. This feature is favorable for catalyzing water oxidation reactions. The generation of oxygen vacancies is inferred to be the result of the interaction between CQD and metal oxides. The Ni 2p and Mo 3d spectra of BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD were subsequently examined, as respectively shown in Fig. 3(d)–(e). The Ni 2p spectrum was featured as 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 orbitals followed by satellite peaks. The Mo 3d spectrum was indicated as 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 orbitals. The binding energies of these peaks are similar in the spectra of BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD. This result suggests that coating CQD has limited influences on the composition and electron states of NiMoO4. Also, the successful growth of NiMoO4 in the BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes was therefore verified. Last, the carbon features of BVO/NM/CQD prepared using different CQD concentrations were examined. The C 1s spectra BVO/NM/CQD prepared using different CQD concentrations were shown in Fig. 3(f). The peaks corresponding to CO, C–O, sp3 C and sp2 C were fitted in all C 1s spectra. The amount of C–O bounds increases and the amount of CO bounds decreases for the BVO/NM/CQD prepared using larger CQD concentrations. This phenomenon suggests the more C–O functional group on CQD than the CO functional group. Both functional groups are inferred to be favorable as active sites for catalyzing water oxidation.

The optical properties of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD were then examined by the UV–vis spectra. The effects of CQD amounts on the optical properties were firstly examined by Fig. 4 (a). The absorption edge at 500 nm is attained in all spectra. This is the optical feature of BiVO4. The incorporations of NiMoO4 and CQD in BVO are likely unable to shift the band gap obviously via creating extra states. The light absorbance is similar for BVO/NM (0 mg/ml) and BVO/NM/CQD with different CQD concentrations, due to the relatively less amounts of CQD covered on the surface. The similar light absorbance with and without CQD coating is favorable for light harvesting. The CQD concentrations used for fabricating BVO/NM/CQD are all favorable for enhancing charge transfer and active site generation without sacrificing the light capture. The band gap of BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD was also evaluated from the Tauc plot (Fig. 4(b)) [51]. The similar band gap of 2.53 eV was obtained for all electrodes. This result again indicates the limited effects on the band structure of BVO/NM with CQD coating. On the other hand, the optical features of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml) were examined by the UV–vis spectra, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The similar absorption edges at 500 nm was again obtained for BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD, implying the limited influences on the band structure of BVO by NiMoO4 and CQD depositions. However, the largely enhanced light absorbance was obtained for BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD compared with that of BVO. Since the light absorbance behavior is similar for BVO and BVO/NM, the enhancement on the light absorbance is likely due to the morphology variation. After carrying out the hydrothermal process for NiMoO4 deposition, many wrinkles appeared on the rod-like structure. The rougher surface may absorb more incident light due to more times of reflection. As a result, higher light absorbance was obtained with incorporation of NiMoO4. Furthermore, the Tauc plot of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD was evaluated to understand the influences of NiMoO4 and CQD on the band gap of BVO (Fig. 4(d)). Again, the similar band gap was obtained for all electrodes. The band structure is inferred to be unchanged after depositing NiMoO4 and CQD on the BVO surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
The photoelectrochemical performance of BVO/NM/CQD electrodes prepared using different CQD concentrations was then examined using the LSV curves, as presented in Fig. 5 (a). At the potential of 1.23 VRHE, the photocurrent densities of 1.4, 1.5, 2.3, 1.9 and 1.8 mA/cm2 were respectively obtained in the LSV curves for BVO/NM/CQD electrodes prepared using 0, 2, 4, 8, and 16 mg/ml. It is noticed that the BVO/NM/CQD electrode prepared using 0 mg/ml is the BVO/NM electrode. With CQD deposition, all the BVO/NM/CQD electrodes show higher photocurrent densities than that of the BVO/NM electrode, implying the feasibility of coating CQD on enhancing the water oxidation reactions. The photocurrent density increases for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode prepared using higher CQD concentration till 4 mg/ml. Further increasing the CQD concentration for preparing the BVO/NM/CQD electrodes lead to the reduction of photocurrent density. The CQD may be the co-catalyst for the BVO/NM system or merely a highly conductive medium to transfer holes more efficiently. The co-catalyst acted as the hole sink for supplying holes to water for carrying out water oxidation reactions. However, too many CQD deposited on the BVO/NM surface could cover the active sites of BVO/NM and reduce its catalytic ability. Therefore, the CQD concentration of 4 mg/ml is regarded as the optimal condition for deposition CQD on the BVO/NM surface. To understand the carrier density in the BVO/NM/CQD electrodes synthesized using different CQD concentrations, the Mott-Schottky plots of BVO/NM/CQD were examined as shown in Fig. 5(b). The Mott-Schottky equation of
1
C 2
=
2
(
V −
V
f b
−
k B
T
e
)
ε
ε 0
A 2
e
N d
(C is differential capacitance,
ε
is dielectric constant of BiVO4,
ε 0
is permittivity of free space, A is area, e is elementary charge, Nd is carrier density, V is applied potential, and
V fb
is flat band potential, kB is Boltzmann constant, and T is absolute temperature) was applied to evaluate the carrier density [52]. The carrier densities of 1.05
×
1014, 1.52
×
1014, 1.66
×
1014, 1.33
×
1014 and 1.31
×
1014 cm−3 were respectively obtained for the BVO/NM/CQD electrodes prepared using 0, 2, 4, 8, and 16 mg/ml. All BVO/NM/CQD electrodes show higher carrier densities than that of the BVO/NM electrode, implying that more carriers can be generated and maintained in the electrode when the CQD was incorporated in BVO/NM systems. The highest carrier density was obtained for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode prepared using 4 mg/ml. This optimal concentration was obtained in considering of the sites for hole sinks and light-to-electron generators. On the other hand, the charge-transfer resistances of BVO/NM/CQD electrodes prepared using 0, 2, 4, 8, and 16 mg/ml were analyzed using the Nyquist plot, as shown in Fig. 5(c). The equivalent circuit was presented in Fig. 5(d) to fit the electric resistances. The charge-transfer resistance (RCT) is the index for the resistance formed at the electrode and electrolyte interface [53]. The RCT values of 237, 210, 185, 180, and 161 Ω were respectively obtained for the BVO/NM/CQD electrodes prepared using 0, 2, 4, 8, and 16 mg/ml. The charge-transfer resistances of the electrodes deposited with CQD are smaller compared to that of the BVO/NM without CQD deposition, owing to the high electric conductivity and extra band state of CQD. Among the BVO/NM/CQD electrodes, the RCT value decreases for the electrode prepared using higher CQD concentrations. Since the better photoelectrochemical performance is not totally obtained for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode prepared using higher CQD concentrations, the charge-transfer resistances in the electrode is not considered to be the dominated factor to determine the water oxidation catalytic ability. Other factors such as the carrier density and the relative sites of hole sinks and light-to-electron generators may have significant influences on the water oxidation catalytic ability.

After obtaining the optimal CQD concentration for fabricating the BVO/NM/CQD electrode, the photoelectrochemical performance of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml) electrodes in the electrolytes without and with the hole scavengers was then examined. Fig. 6 (a)-(c) were measured in the electrolyte without the hole scavenger. Firstly, the transient current plots of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes was measured at 1.23 VRHE, as presented in Fig. 6(a). The current suddenly increases to the maximum value at the light-on moment, due to the excitation of electrons upon light illuminating. During the light-on period, the current continuously decreases to a stable value. The reduction of photocurrent is more obvious at the first several cycles. These spikes are generated because of the charge recombination, which could commonly occur at less active sites such as the impurities and poorly attached catalysts. These less active sites can possibly be consumed after several cycles of scans. On the other hand, the current turns to be zero in a sudden at the light-off moment in all curves, implying the photo-responsive behaviors of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD. The photoelectrochemical performance of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml) electrodes was further examined using the LSV curves, as shown in Fig. 6(b). The photocurrent densities of 1.2, 2.0 and 2.2 mA/cm2 were respectively obtained for the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes. The photocurrent density is largely improved for the electrodes with NiMoO4 depositions, owing to the efficient heterojunction for providing facile charge cascade. With the CQD deposition, the BVO/NM/CQD shows even higher photocurrent density, benefitted from the deposition of CQD acting as the extra hole sinks for raising the water oxidation reactions and as the conductive layer for improving the charge transfer. In addition, the onset potentials of 0.73, 0.52 and 0.48 VRHE were respectively obtained for the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes. The reduction on the onset potential implies the easier initiation of water oxidation. Depositing NiMoO4 for establishing heterojunction can generate facile charge transfer routes, while decorating CQD for developing hole sinks can form efficient water oxidation active sites. As a result, the onset potential was largely decreased due to the incorporation of NiMoO4 and CQD. The Nyquist plots of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes were also measured to evaluate the charge-transfer resistances (Fig. 6(c)). The RCT values of 375, 287 and 185 Ω were respectively obtained for the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes. The smaller charge-transfer resistances of the electrodes incorporated with NiMoO4 and further CQD are again benefitted by the establishments of the heterojunction and the co-catalyst/photocatalyst system.

On the other hand, the photoelectrochemical performance of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD (4 mg/ml) electrodes in the electrolyte with the hole scavengers was then examined. Fig. 6(d)–(f) were measured in the three electrode system with the electrolyte containing the hole scavenger. The transient current plot (Fig. 6(d)) shows almost no spikes in all curves, implying the limited occurrences of charge recombination. The hole scavenger can capture the extra holes and maintain electrons alive in the electrodes. The much higher photocurrent densities of 3.4, 3.5, and 3.9 mA/cm2 were respectively obtained in the LSV curves of the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes (Fig. 6(e)), owing to the exhaustion of extra holes in the electrolyte. The highest photocurrent density was again obtained for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode, but the differences on the photocurrent density between BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD becomes smaller compared to that measured in the electrolyte without the hole scavenger. This phenomenon suggests the better charge recombination reduction ability for the electrodes with NiMoO4 and CQD incorporation. The Nyquist plots of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes were also measured in the electrolyte containing the hole scavenger to evaluate the charge-transfer resistances (Fig. 6(f)). The RCT values of 190, 147 and 119 Ω were respectively obtained for the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes. The smallest charge-transfer resistance was again achieved for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode owing to the developments of heterojunction and co-catalyst. The smaller RCT values for all electrodes were also obtained in the electrolyte containing the hole scavenger compared to those measured in the electrolyte without the hole scavenger, due to the reduction of electron recombination by the additional holes in the electrolyte.

The carrier density and charge recombination in the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes were further examined. The Mott-Schottky plots of BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes were examined as shown in Fig. 7 (a). The carrier densities of 9.58
×
1013, 1.05
×
1014, and 1.66
×
1014 cm−3 were respectively obtained for the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes. The enhancements on the carrier density were achieved by incorporating the NiMoO4 and CQD in BVO. Developing heterojunction and co-catalyst is verified to enhance the carrier density as well. The PL spectra of BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes were examined (Fig. 7(b)). The higher intensity was obtained for the BVO/NM electrode, indicating the more charge recombination occurred in this electrode. Hence, depositing CQD is verified to be able to reduce the charge recombination via providing hole sinks and active sites for water oxidation. Last but not the least, the long-term stability toward water oxidation for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode was examined at 1.23 VRHE. The plot of photocurrent density as a function of time duration was presented in Fig. 7(c). The excellent photocurrent retention of 90% was obtained after 24 h. The SEM and TEM images of BVO/NM/CQD electrode after stability test were presented in Fig. 7(d). The surface of rods is covered by more aggregations, possibly resulted from the debris generated in the ion intercalation and deintercalation process. The XRD pattern of BVO/NM/CQD electrode before and after stability test were shown in Fig. 7(e). The similar patterns indicate the limited changes on the composition after long-term stability test.

The Illustration for the band structure of BVO/NM/CQD and the charge transfer path for water oxidation was presented in Scheme 2 for clearer expressing the mechanism in BVO/NM/CQD. The quasi-triple layered structure was composed of BiVO4, NiMoO4 and CQD. The BiVO4 is the main light absorption layer in which electrons were excited upon light illumination. The holes were left in the valance band of BiVO4. Due to the formation of heterojunction, holes would transfer to the valance band of NiMoO4 and then gather to the hole sink, CQD, to supply for the water oxidation in the electrolyte. The electrons in the conduction band of BiVO4 would transfer to the FTO glass and then to the outer circuit. The function of NiMoO4 is to generate efficient charge cascade and the action of CQD is to play as the hole sink for intensive reactions of water oxidation. As a result, the high photocurrent density, smaller onset potential, less charge-transfer resistance, larger carrier density and the excellent long-term catalytic ability for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode are favorable for the photoelectrochemical water splitting reactions. These results promote the design of BVO/NM/CQD newly proposed in this work to be an excellent catalyst for water oxidation.

The NiMoO4 and CQD were firstly deposited on BVO surface by hydrothermal and soaking processes. The light absorbance was enhanced by incorporating NiMoO4, but the band structure remained unchanged after depositing NiMoO4 and CQD. The optimal CQD concentration of 4 mg/ml for fabricating the BVO/NM/CQD electrode was obtained regarding to the balances on hole sinks and light-to-electron generators amounts. The photocurrent densities of 1.2, 2.0 and 2.2 mA/cm2 were respectively obtained for the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes in the electrolyte without the hole scavenger, while the photocurrent densities of 3.4, 3.5, and 3.9 mA/cm2 were respectively obtained for the BVO, BVO/NM and BVO/NM/CQD electrodes in the electrolyte with the hole scavenger owing to the exhaustion of extra holes in the electrolyte. The BVO/NM/CQD electrode with the heterojunction and co-catalyst designs shows the best photoelectrochemical catalytic ability toward water oxidation with the smallest charge-transfer resistance, onset potential and PL intensity, as well as the highest carrier density. The excellent long-term catalytic ability with the current retention of 90% after 24 h was also attained for the BVO/NM/CQD electrode.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan, under Grant 111-2221-E-027-071-MY3. This work is also assisted by Precision Analysis and Material Research Center, NTUT.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual modification of a BiVO4 photoanode for enhanced photoelectrochemical performance
